title: 1 Corinthians 6
subtitle: Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "The Saints Will Judge the World"

heb.paragraph: The adjective adikos (unrighteous) appears in vv. 1 and 9, creating a wordplay that structures the entire passage. In v. 1, adikoi refers to pagan judges (‘the unrighteous’) before whom believers should not bring lawsuits. In v. 9, adikoi refers to wrongdoers who will not inherit the kingdom. The verbal echo connects the external judicial problem with the internal moral problem: the Corinthians are both wrongly using pagan courts and wrongly behaving in ways that mirror pagan conduct.

The verb apolouō (to wash) in v. 11 is one of three aorist verbs describing the Corinthians’ transformation: ‘you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified.’ The middle voice (apelousasthe) may suggest the believers’ active participation in baptism. The triad — washing, sanctification, justification — encapsulates the entire gospel in a single sentence, moving from cleansing (baptism) through consecration (the Spirit’s work) to legal acquittal (God’s verdict).

refs.note: The saints of the Most High receive authority to judge — the eschatological background for Paul’s claim that believers will judge the world.
Jesus promises the Twelve that they will ‘sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel’ — the dominical source for the saints’ judicial role.
Paul affirms that God justifies the elect — the judicial dimension of salvation that makes worldly courts irrelevant for ultimate justice.

notes.note: The very existence of lawsuits between believers is a defeat (hēttēma). Paul does not address whether the plaintiffs have legitimate grievances; the point is that taking such disputes before unbelievers shames the church. Believers who will judge the world (v. 2) and angels (v. 3) should be capable of resolving petty civil disputes internally.

Paul’s questions are searing: ‘Is it possible that there is nobody among you wise enough to judge a dispute between believers?’ The irony targets a church that prides itself on wisdom (sophia) but cannot manage its own conflicts. Paul then goes further: it would be better to be cheated (adikeisthe) and defrauded (apostereisthe) than to sue a fellow believer.

The vice list is comprehensive: the sexually immoral, idolaters, adulterers, men who have sex with men, thieves, the greedy, drunkards, slanderers, swindlers. Paul’s emphatic ‘that is what some of you were’ (v. 11) establishes both the radical transformation the gospel has effected and the impossibility of returning to the old life.

Three past-tense verbs define the believers’ new identity: washed (baptismal cleansing), sanctified (set apart for God), justified (declared righteous). The order differs from the systematic theological sequence, emphasising experiential priority: baptism was the Corinthians’ point of entry into the new reality.

Calvin distinguishes between the use of civil courts by individuals (which may sometimes be necessary) and the shameful spectacle of believers litigating against each other before pagan judges. The gospel’s reputation is at stake: the world sees Christians unable to resolve their own disputes.

Calvin reads v. 11 as a decisive break: the past (‘that is what some of you were’) is over; the new identity (‘washed, sanctified, justified’) defines the present. He warns that returning to old sins would deny the very transformation that constitutes their Christian identity.
The verb krinō (‘will judge’) in the context of judging the world reflects Jewish apocalyptic traditions (Dan 7:22; Wis 3:8) where the righteous participate in eschatological judgment.

The compound term arsenokoitai (v. 9) combines arsenos (‘male’) and koitē (‘bed’), likely coined from the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13. Its precise scope is debated, but the lexical evidence points to same-sex intercourse as the referent.

Fee argues that the lawsuits reflect the same social stratification driving the divisions of chs. 1–4: wealthier members are using the courts to assert dominance over poorer ones. Paul’s response demolishes the patronage system: in Christ, social hierarchy is irrelevant.

Fee stresses that the vice list is not primarily about boundary-marking but about identity-formation. The emphatic ‘that is what some of you were’ makes the past tense decisive: the Corinthians’ baptismal transformation has placed them in a new reality that excludes the behaviours listed.

Thiselton emphasises that Paul’s argument is eschatological, not merely pragmatic: the saints’ future role as judges of the world makes present submission to pagan courts a contradiction of their eschatological identity.

Thiselton notes the rhetorical force of the adversative alla (‘but’): it marks a decisive contrast between what the Corinthians were and what they now are. The three verbs (washed, sanctified, justified) are not chronologically sequential but theologically complementary perspectives on the same salvific event.

hist.context: Paul addresses another failure of community life: believers taking disputes to pagan courts. In Roman Corinth, litigation was a common tool of social competition. The wealthy routinely used the courts to assert dominance, intimidate rivals, and extract advantage. The Roman legal system favoured the honestiores (upper classes) over the humiliores (lower classes), making the courts an instrument of social stratification. Paul argues that the church — which will judge the world and angels (vv. 2–3) — should be competent to resolve its own disputes. The vice list in vv. 9–10 broadens the argument: those who practice such sins will not inherit God’s kingdom.

sections.header: Verses 12–20 — "Your Bodies Are Temples of the Holy Spirit"

heb.paragraph: The noun sōma (body) appears eight times in vv. 12–20, making it the thematic centre of the passage. In contrast to Greek dualism, which regarded the body as a prison for the soul, Paul insists that the body is ‘for the Lord, and the Lord for the body’ (v. 13). The body is destined for resurrection (v. 14), a member of Christ (v. 15), and a temple of the Holy Spirit (v. 19). This positive theology of the body grounds Paul’s sexual ethics: what one does with the body matters precisely because the body belongs to Christ.

The verb kollaō (to join/cling) in vv. 16–17 denotes a permanent, intimate bond. In the LXX it renders Hebrew dāḇaq (to cling/cleave), used for the marriage bond in Gen 2:24 and for covenant loyalty to God (Deut 10:20). Paul employs it for both sexual union with a prostitute (v. 16) and spiritual union with the Lord (v. 17), making the two bonds mutually exclusive: one who is ‘joined’ to the Lord cannot be ‘joined’ to a prostitute without tearing apart a God-given union.

refs.note: Paul quotes the creation text — ‘the two shall become one flesh’ — to ground his argument that sexual union creates a one-flesh bond.
Paul similarly insists that believers should not let sin reign in their mortal bodies but should offer their bodies as instruments of righteousness.
The resurrection of the body (ch. 15) is the eschatological ground for the body’s present significance (ch. 6). The two passages are theologically inseparable.

notes.note: Paul quotes the Corinthians’ own slogan — ‘I have the right to do anything’ — and qualifies it twice: not everything is beneficial, and nothing should master the believer. Freedom in Christ is not licence but responsible stewardship of the body.

The argument is stunning: believers’ bodies are members of Christ. To unite oneself with a prostitute is to take a member of Christ and make it a member of a prostitute. Paul does not appeal to hygiene, reputation, or social convention but to Christology: the body belongs to Christ.

Paul’s command to ‘flee from sexual immorality’ (pheugete tēn porneian) is abrupt and absolute. Sexual sin is unique (‘against one’s own body’, v. 18) because it violates the Spirit’s temple and contradicts Christ’s ownership. The concluding imperative — ‘honour God with your bodies’ — is the positive counterpart to the negative prohibition.

The body is a temple (naos) of the Holy Spirit. Unlike 3:16, where the temple is the corporate community, here it is the individual believer’s body. Both temples require holiness. The purchase language (‘you were bought at a price’) invokes the metaphor of redemption from the slave market: believers are not their own but belong to the one who purchased them.

Calvin notes that Christian liberty is real but bounded: the believer is free from the ceremonial law but not from moral obligation. Paul’s qualification — ‘I will not be mastered by anything’ — establishes the principle that freedom that enslaves is not freedom at all.

Calvin emphasises the purchase price: believers were bought with the blood of Christ, the most precious payment conceivable. To use the body for sexual immorality is to desecrate the purchase and dishonour the purchaser. The temple metaphor intensifies the obligation: one does not defile a temple.

The phrase panta moi exestin (‘I have the right to do anything’ or ‘all things are lawful for me’) is probably a Corinthian slogan that Paul quotes and qualifies. The NIV’s use of quotation marks reflects this interpretation.

The phrase pan hamārtēma ho ean poiēsē anthrōpos ektos tou sōmatos estin (‘every other sin a person commits is outside the body’) may also be a Corinthian slogan that Paul refutes with his counter-assertion: the sexually immoral person sins against their own body.

Fee argues that the two slogans (‘all things are lawful’ and ‘food for the stomach’) represent the Corinthians’ Spirit-enthusiasm: they believed their pneumatic status made bodily behaviour irrelevant. Paul’s response is that the body matters because it is destined for resurrection and is currently indwelt by the Spirit.

Fee reads v. 19 as the climax of the entire passage: the individual body as a temple of the Holy Spirit grounds an ethic of bodily holiness in pneumatology. For Fee, this is central to Paul’s theology: the Spirit’s indwelling transforms the body into sacred space.

Thiselton argues that the Corinthian slogans reflect a proto-Gnostic devaluation of the body. Paul’s response is thoroughly Jewish-Christian: the body is not irrelevant to salvation but is the very locus of God’s saving work, destined for resurrection by the same power that raised Christ.

Thiselton highlights the chiastic structure: members of Christ (v. 15) / one body with a prostitute (v. 16) / one spirit with the Lord (v. 17) / flee immorality (v. 18) / temple of the Spirit (v. 19). The centre is the contrast between illicit union and legitimate union, framing sexual ethics in relational and covenantal terms.

hist.context: Paul addresses the Corinthians’ misuse of slogans to justify sexual licence. Two apparent quotations from the Corinthians appear: ‘I have the right to do anything’ (panta moi exestin, v. 12) and ‘food for the stomach and the stomach for food’ (v. 13). Some Corinthians were evidently arguing that bodily activities (including sex) were as morally neutral as eating. Paul refutes this by distinguishing between the stomach (which is temporary and will be destroyed) and the body (which is destined for resurrection). Corinth’s famous association with prostitution (both sacred and secular) made this a pressing issue. The temple of Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth, though smaller in Paul’s day than in the classical period, symbolised the city’s reputation.

themes.note: Holiness dominates with the temple-of-the-Spirit climax and the call to flee sexual immorality. Justice scores highly through the lawsuits discussion and the eschatological judgment theme. Judgment remains strong: the saints will judge the world, and wrongdoers will not inherit the kingdom.

rows.label: vv.1–11
rows.text: Lawsuits: The Saints Will Judge the World

rows.label: vv.12–20
rows.text: The Body as Temple: Sexual Ethics Grounded in Christology

lit.note: Chapter 6 addresses two interrelated failures: the use of pagan courts (vv. 1–11) and the misuse of sexual freedom (vv. 12–20). Both reflect the same underlying error: the Corinthians have failed to grasp the implications of their new identity in Christ for bodily conduct.

hebtext.note: The adjective adikos (unrighteous) appears in vv. 1 and 9, creating a wordplay that structures the entire passage. In v. 1, adikoi refers to pagan judges (‘the unrighteous’) before whom believers should not bring lawsuits. In v. 9, adikoi refers to wrongdoers who will not inherit the kingdom. The verbal echo connects the external judicial problem with the internal moral problem: the Corinthians are both wrongly using pagan courts and wrongly behaving in ways that mirror pagan conduct.

The verb apolouō (to wash) in v. 11 is one of three aorist verbs describing the Corinthians’ transformation: ‘you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified.’ The middle voice (apelousasthe) may suggest the believers’ active participation in baptism. The triad — washing, sanctification, justification — encapsulates the entire gospel in a single sentence, moving from cleansing (baptism) through consecration (the Spirit’s work) to legal acquittal (God’s verdict).

The noun sōma (body) appears eight times in vv. 12–20, making it the thematic centre of the passage. In contrast to Greek dualism, which regarded the body as a prison for the soul, Paul insists that the body is ‘for the Lord, and the Lord for the body’ (v. 13). The body is destined for resurrection (v. 14), a member of Christ (v. 15), and a temple of the Holy Spirit (v. 19). This positive theology of the body grounds Paul’s sexual ethics: what one does with the body matters precisely because the body belongs to Christ.

The verb kollaō (to join/cling) in vv. 16–17 denotes a permanent, intimate bond. In the LXX it renders Hebrew dāḇaq (to cling/cleave), used for the marriage bond in Gen 2:24 and for covenant loyalty to God (Deut 10:20). Paul employs it for both sexual union with a prostitute (v. 16) and spiritual union with the Lord (v. 17), making the two bonds mutually exclusive: one who is ‘joined’ to the Lord cannot be ‘joined’ to a prostitute without tearing apart a God-given union.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: The gospel’s reputation is at stake: the world sees Christians unable to resolve their own disputes…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple

translations.text: Therefore, if you have disputes about such matters, do you ask for a ruling from those whose way of life is scorned in the church?
So if you have such cases, why do you lay them before those who have no standing in the church?
Instead, you yourselves cheat and do wrong, and you do this to your brothers and sisters.
But you yourselves wrong and defraud—even your own brothers!

thread.anchor: Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple
thread.text: The saints of the Most High receive authority to judge — the eschatological background for Paul’s claim that believers will judge the world.

thread.text: Jesus promises the Twelve that they will ‘sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel’ — the dominical source for the saints’ judicial role.

thread.anchor: Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple
thread.text: Paul affirms that God justifies the elect — the judicial dimension of salvation that makes worldly courts irrelevant for ultimate justice.

thread.anchor: Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple
thread.text: Paul similarly insists that believers should not let sin reign in their mortal bodies but should offer their bodies as instruments of righteousness.

thread.anchor: Lawsuits Among Believers and the Body as Temple
thread.text: The resurrection of the body (ch. 15) is the eschatological ground for the body’s present significance (ch. 6). The two passages are theologically inseparable.

discourse.thesis: Lawsuits between believers expose a failure of wisdom and a misunderstanding of the body — your bodies belong to the Lord, temples of the Holy Spirit, purchased at a price; therefore glorify God in your body.

nodes.text: Dare any of you take a grievance before unrighteous judges rather than before the saints? Do you not know that the saints will judge the world — and even angels? If you judge the world, are you incompetent for trivial cases? To have lawsuits at all is a failure — why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?

The list of those who will not inherit the kingdom: sexually immoral, idolaters, adulterers, men who practice homosexuality, thieves, greedy, drunkards, revilers, swindlers. And such were some of you — but you were washed, sanctified, justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and in the Spirit of our God.

children.text: The 'but you were washed' is the ethical foundation: identity change precedes behavioral change. The three passive verbs (washed, sanctified, justified) describe what has been done to them — the imperative rests on the indicative.

nodes.text: On sexual morality: 'All things are lawful' (a Corinthian slogan Paul quotes back) — but not all things are helpful. The body is not for sexual immorality but for the Lord. Joining oneself to a prostitute makes one body with her (Gen 2:24 — 'the two will become one flesh'). Flee (φεύγετε) sexual immorality — every other sin is outside the body, but sexual immorality is against one's own body.

children.text: Do you not know that your body is a temple (ναός) of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own — you were bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in your body. The three grounds: Spirit-inhabitation, purchased ownership, divine design — all point to bodily holiness.

discourse.note: 1 Corinthians 6 addresses two apparently unrelated issues (lawsuits, sexual morality) that share a common root: misunderstanding of the body and the believer's new identity. Paul's 'do you not know' formula (vv. 2, 3, 9, 15, 16, 19) is pedagogical — he is not providing new information but awakening the community to implications of what they already know.
